data.title: Cibus outlines advanced gene editing initiatives with potential revenue milestones by 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cibus, Inc. (CBUS) Q4 2024

Management View

  • Interim CEO Peter Beetham emphasized the company's strategic transformation from an agricultural trait development company to a commercial gene editing company. He highlighted the progress in commercializing gene-edited traits, particularly in rice and canola, leveraging their Rapid Trait Development System (RTDS).

  • The company affirmed its collaboration with RTDC Corporation Limited and Albaugh LLC to provide herbicide Clethodim for U.S. rice farmers using the HT3 trait. Agreements with four major rice seed companies in North and Latin America now represent approximately 40% of estimated accessible rice acres in these regions.

  • Progress was reported on advanced canola traits for Pod Shatter Reduction and for resistance to diseases such as Sclerotinia. A notable partnership with Biographica was announced to leverage AI for novel gene discovery.

  • CFO Carlo Broos reported that cash and cash equivalents were $14.4 million as of December 31, 2024, with expectations to fund operations into late Q3 2025 following cost-saving initiatives.

  • The company plans further development of its soybean platform, targeting traits like herbicide tolerance (HT2) and white mold resistance. These initiatives aim to address a market of 125 million soybean acres.

Outlook

  • The company projects the completion of field trials and regulatory approvals for the HT2 trait in canola with expected data by Q3 2025. Initial commercial launches for canola traits are planned for 2026.

  • Current commercial efforts in rice anticipate launches in Latin America by 2027, with the U.S. following by 2028, supported by new herbicide registration collaborations.

  • Regulatory harmonization in the EU and other regions is expected to open new markets for gene-edited traits, enabling further expansion.

Financial Results

  • R&D expenses for Q4 2024 decreased to $12.4 million from $14.2 million in the prior year, attributed to cost-saving initiatives and lower stock compensation expenses.

  • SG&A expenses remained steady at $6.8 million year-over-year. The net loss for Q4 2024 was $25.8 million, showing improvement when excluding the prior year's $249 million goodwill impairment.

Q&A

  • Laurence Alexander, Jefferies, asked about the EU regulatory progress and its impact on commercialization timelines. Interim CEO Beetham stressed the importance of pending EU legislation for harmonizing global regulations. He noted that this progress supports accelerated customer programs.

  • Austin Moeller, Canaccord, inquired about commercialization timelines. Beetham confirmed that HT2 field trials and regulatory steps are on track for canola, with launches expected by 2026.

  • Sameer Joshi, H.C. Wainwright, raised questions about sustainable ingredients and biofragrance revenues. Beetham projected initial revenues by 2026, citing progress in fermentation-based product development.

  • Matthew Venezia, Alliance Global Partners, asked about prioritization among traits and crops. Beetham highlighted focus areas in rice, canola, and soybean platforms, all aligned with strategic commercialization goals.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism about regulatory advancements and commercialization timelines, noting progress in rice and canola traits. Questions reflected a keen interest in revenue generation and market expansion.

  • Management demonstrated confidence, emphasizing achievements in efficiency and regulatory alignment. Beetham's responses underscored a strategic focus on meeting key milestones.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 2024 saw a continued emphasis on regulatory progress and commercialization timelines, particularly in Europe and North America. This builds on Q3 2024's focus on operationalizing the Trait Machine process.

  • Financial improvements were noted, with ongoing cost reductions contributing to a more sustainable cash flow outlook compared to Q3.

  • The sentiment was consistent, with management maintaining a focus on milestone achievements and analysts seeking clarity on strategic timelines and financial viability.

Risks and Concerns

  • The company flagged potential challenges in securing regulatory approvals and scaling operations. Management remains optimistic, citing partnerships and cost management as mitigation strategies.

  • Analysts raised concerns about cash flow sustainability beyond Q3 2025 and the timing of commercial launches.

Final Takeaway

Cibus is advancing its commercialization efforts for gene-edited traits with significant progress in rice and canola, supported by regulatory harmonization and strategic collaborations. The company is focused on achieving critical milestones in 2025 and 2026 while addressing operational and financial challenges through cost-saving measures and partnerships. These developments position Cibus to capitalize on the growing demand for innovative agricultural solutions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Ouster outlines $19B smart infrastructure market potential for 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ouster, Inc. (OUST) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Angus Pacala highlighted a record quarter, with $30 million in revenue and 44% gross margins, marking the eighth consecutive quarter of meeting or exceeding guidance. He emphasized Ouster's strong balance sheet, with $175 million in cash and no debt.
  • The company secured a $2 million contract for its Blue City traffic management solution in Chattanooga, Tennessee, marking the largest deployment of lidar detection technology for traffic and pedestrian safety in the U.S.
  • The Blue City solution has been launched across the U.S., targeting the Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) market, estimated at over 300,000 intersections domestically and of similar scale in Europe and Asia.
  • Interim CFO Chen Geng reported that Ouster shipped approximately 4,800 sensors in the fourth quarter, driven by large-volume purchases from automotive and robotics sectors, with continued strength in industrial applications.

Outlook

  • Management provided Q1 2025 revenue guidance of $30 million to $32 million.
  • CEO Pacala outlined three strategic priorities for 2025: scaling the software-attached business, transforming the product portfolio, and progressing towards profitability. He emphasized capturing the $19 billion smart infrastructure market opportunity and expanding penetration in security and logistics applications.
  • The company plans to introduce new hardware and software features, including 3D Zone Monitoring and a cloud-based portal for the Ouster Gemini platform, to enhance usability and drive new revenue streams.
  • Management reaffirmed its long-term framework of 30% to 50% annual revenue growth, gross margins of 35% to 40%, and operating expenses at or below Q3 2023 levels.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q4 2024 reached $30 million, supported by strong sensor shipments and a diverse customer base across automotive, robotics, and industrial sectors.
  • Gross margins improved to 44%, driven by record sensor volumes and lower costs related to legacy inventory.
  • Full-year revenue for 2024 was $111 million, representing a 33% year-over-year increase, with gross margins of 36%.
  • Operating expenses in Q4 2024 were $39 million, reflecting a focus on cost control and a 7% reduction in average quarterly operating expenses compared to Q3 2023 levels.

Q&A

  • Andres Sheppard, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about the use of sensors in robotaxis and last-mile delivery. CEO Pacala noted the resurgence in the robotaxi market led by companies like Waymo and increased adoption in industrial robotics, driven by advancements in physical AI.
  • Timothy Savageaux, Northland Capital Markets: Inquired about the competitive landscape and Ouster's position as a Western lidar supplier. CEO Pacala emphasized the company's strategic supply chain outside China and its differentiation in markets like smart infrastructure.
  • Casey Ryan, WestPark Capital: Questioned gross margin variability and pricing competitiveness in smart infrastructure. Interim CFO Geng highlighted that software-attached businesses provide accretive margins and CEO Pacala noted that pricing is competitive but linked to performance and capability advantages.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about competitive dynamics and pricing pressures but recognized the company's strong financial performance and strategic expansion.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing growth opportunities and technological differentiation.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management showed increased assertiveness in outlining growth initiatives and addressing competitive questions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue increased from $28 million in Q3 2024 to $30 million in Q4 2024, supported by higher sensor shipments and robust demand across key sectors.
  • Gross margins expanded from 38% to 44%, reflecting improved product mix and reduced costs.
  • Management's tone shifted to a more forward-looking focus, with clear strategic priorities for 2025 highlighted.
  • Analysts continued to emphasize competitive dynamics and market penetration, consistent with the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted potential impacts from evolving tariff environments but stated that the supply chain is well-positioned to mitigate risks.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about pricing pressures and competitive dynamics in key markets.
  • Management aims to address risks through product innovation and targeted market penetration strategies.

Final Takeaway

Ouster concluded 2024 with strong financial performance and laid out an ambitious roadmap for 2025, focusing on scaling its software-attached business, expanding its product portfolio, and capturing significant market opportunities in smart infrastructure and robotics. With a robust balance sheet and clear strategic priorities, the company aims to enhance its competitive position and progress toward profitability.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Motorsport Games targets console expansion and subscription growth in 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Motorsport Games Inc. (MSGM) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Stephen Hood highlighted the successful updates to "Le Mans Ultimate," which significantly increased player engagement and sales. He noted the February 25 update led to a substantial spike in active player numbers and sales, with improving retention metrics indicating players shifting from competitor platforms.

  • The company launched a subscription service via RaceControl.gg, generating over $120,000 in revenue in the initial 21 days of the accounting period. As of the earnings call, the service had nearly 6,000 active subscribers, achieved without any paid marketing efforts.

  • Hood disclosed plans to explore console ports for "Le Mans Ultimate," aiming to bring the game to PlayStation and Xbox platforms. He emphasized this as a response to player demand and a strategic step following the game's success on PC.

  • CFO Stanley Beckley detailed the Q4 2024 revenue increase to $2.0 million, primarily driven by digital game sales of "Le Mans Ultimate." However, the net loss for the quarter stood at $2.9 million, attributed to increased other expenses and the absence of one-time income seen in the prior year.

Outlook

  • Management expressed confidence in the ongoing momentum of "Le Mans Ultimate" and indicated readiness for further updates, including livery creation and driver swap features, which are set to enhance the game’s appeal and support large-scale eSports events.

  • Hood mentioned ongoing discussions with potential investors, publishers, and partners for future projects, signaling robust interest in the company’s offerings.

Financial Results

  • Quarterly revenue increased to $2.0 million, driven by "Le Mans Ultimate" sales, up from $1.8 million in Q3 2024. Full-year revenue reached $8.7 million, a $1.8 million increase compared to 2023, attributed largely to the release and updates of "Le Mans Ultimate."

  • The net loss for Q4 2024 was $2.9 million, compared to a $0.6 million loss in Q3 2024, with the decline stemming from higher other expenses and the absence of one-time gains seen in the prior year.

  • Adjusted EBITDA loss for Q4 2024 was $2.5 million, widening from a $0.1 million loss in Q3 2024. Management stated this was due to similar factors that influenced the net loss.

Q&A

  • NOT_APPLICABLE: There was no formal Q&A session during this earnings call.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was not directly observable due to the absence of a Q&A session.

  • Management maintained a confident tone during the prepared remarks, emphasizing strategic progress and positive market reception of "Le Mans Ultimate." Hood’s statements reflected optimism about the company’s future, particularly in leveraging new revenue streams and platform expansions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue grew from $1.8 million in Q3 2024 to $2.0 million in Q4 2024, driven by continued sales of "Le Mans Ultimate."

  • Net loss widened from $0.6 million in Q3 2024 to $2.9 million in Q4 2024, attributed to increased expenses and the lack of one-time income.

  • Management’s tone remained positive, building on the momentum from Q3, but with more emphasis on leveraging partnerships and platform expansion in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • CFO Beckley highlighted liquidity challenges, stating the company’s cash position is insufficient to fund operations over the next year without additional funding. Management is exploring equity, debt financing, and other strategic options to address this issue.

  • Legal and operational challenges were noted, though management pointed out favorable outcomes in recent litigations and settlements, which have improved the company’s financial position.

Final Takeaway

Motorsport Games Inc. highlighted significant progress in product enhancement and subscriber acquisition, particularly with "Le Mans Ultimate," while addressing financial challenges and exploring new growth opportunities, including console expansion and partnerships. Management remains optimistic about the company’s trajectory, supported by ongoing player engagement and strategic initiatives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Academy sports outlines 20–25 new store openings for 2025 with focus on growth through new brands and e-commerce
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Academy Sports and Outdoors (ASO) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Steve Lawrence highlighted that Q4 net sales reached $1.68 billion, down 6.6% year-over-year due to the prior year's 53-week comparison. On a comparable 13-week basis, Q4 net sales were flat, with comp sales down 3% excluding new stores, showing sequential improvement from earlier in the year.
  • The outdoor category was the top performer, with 2% net sales growth, driven by hunting, fishing, and camping. Apparel rebounded, supported by strong holiday sales in youth apparel and workwear categories, with significant contributions from brands like Nike and Carhartt.
  • Private brands accounted for 23% of total sales in 2024, up from 22% in 2023, indicating growing customer preference for value-focused offerings.
  • CFO Carl Ford reported gross margin at 32.2%, down 110 basis points year-over-year, impacted by higher freight and distribution costs. Merchandise margins increased by 10 basis points.
  • Lawrence announced ambitious plans to launch the Jordan brand in 145 stores and online in Q1 2025, marking the company's largest brand launch to date. The company also plans to open 20–25 new stores in 2025, expanding into Pennsylvania and Maryland, bringing total state coverage to 21.

Outlook

  • Net sales for 2025 are projected to range from $6.1 billion to $6.3 billion, with comparable sales guidance of negative 2% to positive 1%. Gross margin rate is expected between 34.0% and 34.5%.
  • Diluted earnings per share are anticipated to be $5.40 to $5.85, with adjusted EPS forecasted between $5.75 and $6.20.
  • The company expects Q1 2025 to be the most challenging quarter due to upfront costs for new store openings and brand launches, with Q2 set to benefit from these investments.

Financial Results

  • Academy Sports reported Q4 net income of $133.6 million, with diluted EPS of $1.89. Adjusted net income was $139 million, or $1.96 per share.
  • SG&A expenses increased by 110 basis points, driven by strategic investments in new stores and omnichannel capabilities.
  • Inventory levels ended Q4 at $1.3 billion, up 9.6% year-over-year. Per-store inventory units decreased by 2.9%.

Q&A

  • Analyst Christopher Horvers, JPMorgan, asked about gross margin guidance drivers amid tariff pressures. CFO Carl Ford attributed the forecasted 40 basis-point expansion to recapturing supply chain efficiencies and a favorable mix shift towards softlines, supported by promotional strategies.
  • Analyst Brian Nagel, Oppenheimer, questioned the scope of the Jordan launch. CEO Lawrence emphasized a broad rollout across apparel, footwear, and sporting goods, with a focus on underserved markets.
  • Analyst Kate McShane, Goldman Sachs, inquired about customer demographic shifts. CFO Ford noted increased traffic from higher-income cohorts, reflecting a broader appeal of value-driven offerings.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed cautious optimism about the guidance for 2025, with a focus on the effectiveness of new store openings and brand launches.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, citing robust strategic plans and improved inventory management. The sentiment during the Q&A was positive, with detailed responses indicating preparedness to navigate macroeconomic challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 showed sequential improvement in comp sales from Q3. Gross margin compression narrowed from Q3’s 50 basis points to 110 basis points in Q4 on a year-over-year basis.
  • Strategic focus shifted from immediate operational challenges like supply chain disruptions in Q3 to long-term growth initiatives in Q4, including the Jordan launch and diversified store openings.
  • Analyst focus evolved from weather impacts and demand fluctuations in Q3 to assessing brand and store portfolio expansions in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified macroeconomic uncertainty and tariff impacts as key challenges for 2025. Diversification of sourcing and price optimization were highlighted as mitigation strategies.
  • Analysts raised concerns about potential softness in consumer spending and its impact on Q1 2025 performance, given anticipated upfront costs.

Final Takeaway

Academy Sports and Outdoors demonstrated resilience in Q4 2024 with improved sales trends and robust strategic initiatives, including a major brand launch and expanded store footprint. With a measured but optimistic outlook for 2025, the company focuses on driving growth through new store openings, enhanced omnichannel capabilities, and strategic brand partnerships, positioning itself to capture market share amid macro challenges.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Alnylam nabs key additional indication for Amvuttra
data.description:
• The U.S. FDA on Thursday approved Alnylam Pharmaceuticals' (NASDAQ:ALNY) application to add cardiomyopathy of wild-type or hereditary transthyretin-mediated amyloidosis (ATTR-CM) to the labeling of its RNAi therapy Amvuttra (vutrisiran).
  • The treatment was already approved for polyneuropathy of hereditary transthyretin-mediated amyloidosis (hATTR-PN).
  • The biopharma said that the therapy is the only one available in the U.S. to treat cardiomyopathy and polyneuropathy manifestations of ATTR amyloidosis.
  • With the new indication, Amvuttra will compete against BridgeBio Pharma's (NASDAQ:BBIO) Attruby (acoramidis) and Pfizer's (NYSE:PFE) Vyndamax (tafamidis).

data.title: Accenture faces revenue pressure amid Trump crackdown on consultants
data.description:
Accenture (NYSE:ACN), a major consulting firm, warned investors about financial pressures due to the government’s push to cut federal contracting costs. The Trump administration plans to intensify these efforts by requiring consulting firms, including Accenture (NYSE:ACN), IBM (IBM) and Deloitte, to justify their federal work through a scorecard system, The Wall Street Journal reported.

A General Services Administration letter instructs firms to review existing contracts, identify waste, and simplify explanations of their services. The GSA insists on pricing concessions and clarity, stating that a "15-year-old should understand" the work being done. Companies must submit their findings by March 31.

Accenture’s (ACN) stock dropped over 7% following CEO Julie Sweet’s comments about government spending cuts slowing new contracts and impacting revenue. Other firms, such as IBM (IBM) and Booz Allen (BAH), also saw stock declines.

The administration's cost-cutting push has led to industry concerns and meetings with GSA official Josh Gruenbaum, a former KKR executive. Firms must justify their projects’ necessity, with the GSA warning that failure to identify waste could be seen as resistance to the administration’s goals.

Despite the scrutiny, Sweet defended Accenture’s (ACN) role, arguing that the company helps modernize and streamline government operations for efficiency. However, the GSA made it clear: not all projects will be considered essential, the Journal reported.

data.title: Torrid Holdings outlines 2025 growth strategy with 7% to 10% sub-brand investment
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Torrid Holdings Inc. (CURV) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Lisa Harper highlighted the company's strong performance, exceeding expectations with $275.6 million in sales and $16.7 million in adjusted EBITDA for Q4 2024. She emphasized the positive customer response to sub-brands and the strategic focus on enhancing product offerings, driving customer growth, and optimizing the retail footprint.
  • Harper detailed the rollout of three new sub-brands: Festi, Nightfall, and Retro Chic. These sub-brands have driven significant customer engagement, especially among younger demographics, with a reported increase in mid-30s average age for new customers.
  • CFO Paula Dempsey emphasized the company's improved financial position, ending the year with $48.5 million in cash, a significant rise from the prior year. She attributed this growth to disciplined cost control and successful inventory management.

Outlook

  • The company projects full-year 2025 sales between $1.080 billion and $1.100 billion, supported by ongoing product innovation and strategic initiatives.
  • Adjusted EBITDA is forecasted to range from $100 million to $110 million. For Q1 2025, sales are expected between $264 million and $274 million, with adjusted EBITDA projected at $24 million to $28 million.
  • Management plans to close 40 to 50 stores in fiscal 2025, focusing on enhancing profitability and aligning with customer preferences for outdoor shopping centers.
  • Sub-brands will account for approximately 7% to 10% of the receipt investment for 2025, signaling a strategic shift to broaden product offerings.

Financial Results

  • Q4 2024 reported sales of $275.6 million, a slight year-over-year increase when excluding the extra week in fiscal 2023. Comparable sales showed a decline of 0.8%, with a marked improvement in regular price comps at 1.6%.
  • Gross margin was 33.6%, reflecting a minor decline due to lower sales volume but showing improvement in product margins year-over-year.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter reached $16.7 million, up from $16.4 million in the prior year, demonstrating effective cost management despite macroeconomic pressures.
  • The company ended the fiscal year with $148.5 million in inventory, up 4% due to in-transit goods but down 18% on a two-year basis, showcasing improved inventory discipline.

Q&A

  • Brooke Roach, Goldman Sachs: Questioned the health of Torrid's customer base and the projected contribution of sub-brands to comps. CEO Harper detailed the fundamental strength of the customer base, noting strong traffic trends and engagement in core categories like denim and dresses.
  • Dylan Carden, William Blair: Asked about the long-term outlook for store closures. CFO Dempsey clarified that closures would primarily occur in Q4 2025, with minimal near-term revenue impact but expected margin benefits in fiscal 2026.
  • Corey Tarlowe, Jefferies: Inquired about promotional and clearance strategies. Harper explained the moderation of clearance sales impacts and strategic adjustments to promotional activities to align with customer behavior trends.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed cautious optimism, probing the sustainability of customer engagement and the financial impact of strategic initiatives. Questions were focused on macroeconomic pressures and operational execution.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing financial stability, strategic innovation, and positive early signs from new product launches. Harper's remarks demonstrated assurance in the company’s ability to navigate uncertainties.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, there was a noticeable shift in tone, with increased confidence in the product pipeline and customer response to new initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 2024 saw an improvement in regular price comps and a decrease in the negative impact of clearance sales compared to Q3. The launch of sub-brands marked a significant strategic advancement.
  • Management’s tone shifted from addressing past product challenges to focusing on growth and innovation for 2025.
  • Analysts' focus transitioned from inventory and margin management in Q3 to the success of sub-brands and customer acquisition strategies in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged macroeconomic uncertainties and consumer spending variability as ongoing challenges. Harper emphasized the need for cautious optimism and prudent spending.
  • Analysts raised concerns about potential risks associated with store closures and the scaling of new sub-brands. Management highlighted the use of data-driven insights and flexible strategies to mitigate these risks.

Final Takeaway

Torrid Holdings demonstrated resilience and strategic focus in Q4 2024, with strong financial execution and promising early results from new sub-brands. The company’s emphasis on product innovation, customer engagement, and operational efficiency positions it for sustainable growth in fiscal 2025, despite navigating a challenging macroeconomic environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gold futures add to record in eight-day win streak; Citi sees gold up to $3,500 by year-end
data.description:
Gold futures eked out another record high on Thursday, marking the eighth straight session that front-month gold settled higher, climbing 5% over that span.

Gold buyers were encouraged by the dovish tone from the Federal Reserve and Chair Jerome Powell's press conference, which backed expectations of two interest rate cuts this year, Trade Nation's David Morrison said.

However, gold pared gains earlier in the session in a sudden risk-off move, which Morrison said shows investors are getting more nervous about gold's next move, with some believing it has gone up too far and too fast, adding that it would not be a surprise to see a downward correction in prices.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for March delivery finished +0.1% to a new record high settlement of $3,040.00/oz, but front-month March silver (XAGUSD:CUR) ended -0.5% to $33.786/oz, its third loss in four sessions.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (NYSEARCA:GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

Citi Research raised its price forecast for gold futures to $3,200/oz within the next three months, up $200/oz from the firm's previous outlook, possibly climbing as high as $3,500 by the end of the year if fears of growing U.S. economic woes take hold.

"In our bull case, we see gold prices reaching $3,500/oz by year-end, underpinned by much higher hedging/investment demand on fears of U.S. hard landing/stagflation," Citi said.

"If President Trump's plan to lower energy prices fails to sufficiently offset tariff-driven inflation, U.S. labor market deterioration accelerates, and equity market drawdown continues, elevated levels of household fear... can drive gold price to reach new records," the bank wrote.

data.title: Fading Warren Buffett: Citi downgrades Constellation Brands
data.description:
Constellation Brands (NYSE:STZ) continues to be looked at cautiously by analysts, amid concerns about the impact of tariffs on earnings and broad industry trends. 

On Thursday, Citi downgraded Citi to a Buy from Neutral as it pointed to the persistent slowdown in beer sales. The beer sales slowdown was attributed to weakness in the Hispanic consumer base, poor weather, and a soft U.S. beer category, which could make it harder for the beverage giant to gain shelf space as brand velocities slow.

The company's beer business was noted to have grown at an average of about 12% since 2014, driven by strong distribution gains, innovation, and growth among Hispanic consumers. However, recent depletions have slowed to around 3% in the last two quarters, with a forecasted decline of 2% in the fourth quarter of 2025, warned analyst Filippo Falorni.

Looking ahead, Citi believes the sales weakness could lead to lower medium-term growth targets, potentially reducing capacity utilization and negatively impacting beer margins. The company's valuation is currently around 13X EPS, which is below historical averages, but Citi sees little chance of a re-rating due to looming tariff risks that could significantly impact earnings. As a result, Citi has lowered its target price for STZ to $200 from $260 to $200. 

On a longer timeline, Warren Buffett is a strong believer in Constellation Brands (NYSE:STZ). Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) acquired a $1.24 billion stake in the company earlier in the month. The investment consisted of 5.6 million shares of Constellation Brands, making Berkshire Hathaway the sixth-largest shareholder in the beverage conglomerate.

Constellation Brands' (NYSE:STZ) portfolio aligns well with Buffett's investment philosophy, which often focuses on companies with strong brands, consistent earnings growth, and durable competitive advantages. The company's beer segment, in particular, has shown strong performance over the last few years.

Shares of Constellation Brands (STZ) traded flat on Thursday. For the year, the stock is down by more than 17%. The Seeking Alpha Quant Rating on STZ is flashing Sell, while Seeking Alpha analysts and Wall Street analysts still have a consensus Buy rating on the stock.

data.title: Former Texas congressman Burgess eyed as CDC director nominee - report
data.description:
• The White House is reportedly considering former U.S. Rep. Michael Burgess (R-Texas) as its nominee to head the CDC.
  • Burgess, an ob/gyn by training, served in the House of Representatives from January 2023-January 2025.
  • He is likely to be nominated, though a final decision has yet to be made, Reuters reported, citing sources.
  • The Trump administration pulled the nomination of its first candidate, David Weldon, earlier this month, hours before he was to appear before a Senate confirmation hearing, allegedly because of his prior anti-vaccine comments.
  • In an October 2020 post on X, Burgess praised President Trump's handling of the COVID-19 pandemic during his first term. "In my 17 years in congress, I’ve seen several novel infectious diseases become public health concerns, but I have never seen an administration work harder than [Trump's] to unleash the public and private sectors to expand testing, deliver PPE, and develop a vaccine."
  • Vaccine names: Merck (NYSE:MRK), GSK (NYSE:GSK), Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY), Moderna (NASDAQ:MRNA), BioNTech (NASDAQ:BNTX), Pfizer (NYSE:PFE), and Novavax (NASDAQ:NVAX).
